Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Julian of Norwich

1343 – after 1416

Divine love, inward vision, and the soul’s healing union with God.

Julian of Norwich’s implicit philosophy of mind is best described as a participatory, incarnational, affective-cognitive philosophy of the soul. The mind is not, for her, an isolated private mechanism. It is the human soul as created, loved, embodied, changeable, wounded, remembered by God, and capable of being “oned” with God. Its deepest structure is not self-enclosed subjectivity but participation: the soul’s “substance” is grounded in God, while its “sensuality” is its embodied, temporal, affective, and changeable life. Cognition is therefore never merely propositional. It is seeing, beholding, understanding, feeling, remembering, willing, longing, loving, and being healed.

This also means that the “mind of God” is not a decorative theological background. Julian’s whole account of human mentality depends on divine cognition: God’s “foreseeing wisdom,” eternal love, non-wrathful judgment, providential ordering, and maternal mercy constitute the metaphysical environment in which human minds can be understood at all. Nor are angels irrelevant. Julian gives no systematic angelology, but she affirms angelic ministration as part of the received Christian order while insisting that Christ is nearer and more fundamental to the human soul than any angelic mediator.

1. Textual and methodological frame

Julian’s visions occurred in May 1373, when she was about thirty and a half and gravely ill. Two accounts survive: a shorter account and a much longer version composed after years of inward reflection. The Middle English Texts Series introduction notes that the long text is marked by “a bolder and a more elaborated theology,” and that Julian herself distinguishes the modes of the revelation: bodily sight, words formed in the understanding, and “ghostly” or spiritual sight.

This matters philosophically. Julian is not simply reporting mystical experiences and then adding doctrine afterward. Her text is a sustained reflection on modes of perception, forms of understanding, the relation between affect and cognition, the structure of selfhood, the causes of sin, the possibility of self-knowledge, and the way divine and human willing interpenetrate without collapsing into identity. Her work is thus legitimate material for a philosophy of mind, provided we avoid two errors: first, reducing her thought to modern psychology; second, treating the theological dimensions as non-philosophical. In Julian, theology is not an external theme added to the mind; it is the metaphysical condition of mind.

A recent Cambridge summary emphasizes that the longer version of the Revelations develops a “woman-centred exposition of deep theological sophistication,” suggesting extensive learning. That sophistication is especially visible in chapters 44–57 and 80–86 of the long text, where Julian’s anthropology, psychology, Christology, epistemology, and doctrine of divine love become tightly interwoven.

2. Julian’s vocabulary of mind

Julian does not have one term that maps neatly onto the modern word “mind.” Her philosophy of mind must be reconstructed from a cluster of terms and operations.

Her most important term is soule, but “soul” in Julian should not be read as a thinly disembodied substance. The soul includes both substance and sensuality. “Substance” is the deep, created, God-grounded identity of the person; “sensuality” is the embodied, temporal, affective, changeable, vulnerable life of the soul. Julian says that substance and sensuality together “may rightly be called our soul,” and that God is present in sensuality as well as in substance.

The term reson or reason is not secular calculation. Julian calls reason grounded in God and identifies it as one of the highest gifts given to human beings. Yet reason alone is insufficient: it must be joined with “mind” and love. The result is a psychology in which intelligence, memory/attention, and charity cannot be cleanly separated.

Her understonding is receptive and illuminative. Words can be “formed” in the understanding; insight can arrive as vision, as inwardly articulated speech, or as spiritual sight beyond ordinary representation.

Her will is not mere appetite. The METS notes explain that in medieval psychology the will is the faculty that chooses and loves, and that Julian’s “will” and “willful” often indicate sustained intentionality or fully conscious choosing. Julian develops this into a powerful distinction between a lower “beastly” will and a higher “godly will” that never assents to sin.

Finally, her words for sight, beholding, feeling, longing, cleaving, dread, hope, and joy are not merely emotional color. They are cognitive-affective modes. “Beholding” is a directed act of contemplative attention. “Feeling” can be epistemic, not just somatic. “Longing” is an intentional orientation toward the soul’s proper end.

3. The ontology of the soul: created, God-grounded, not self-grounded

Julian’s most fundamental claim about the soul is that it is created directly by God and grounded in God. She distinguishes the making of the body from the making of the soul: the body is made from earthly matter, but the soul is made “of nought,” meaning not from any already-created matter. In the same passage she says that the soul is made by God and immediately knit to God.

This is not materialism, because the soul is not reducible to bodily matter. It is also not Cartesian dualism, because the soul’s embodied “sensuality” is not an accidental prison or mere external vehicle. Nor is it pantheism, because Julian explicitly clarifies the apparent identity between God and the soul: she sees “no difference” between God and the soul’s substance “but as it were all God,” yet immediately explains that God is God and the soul’s substance is a creature in God.

This gives us Julian’s first major philosophical thesis: the human mind is a created participation in divine being, not an autonomous self-subsisting mental atom. The mind’s deepest identity is relational before it is introspective. It is “in God” before it is “in itself.”

This is why Julian can also say that it is easier to come to the knowledge of God than to know one’s own soul. The soul is so deeply grounded in God that self-knowledge requires seeking the soul where it actually is: in God. She even says God is nearer to us than our own soul, because God is the ground in which the soul stands and the medium that keeps substance and sensuality together.

A Spanish Catholic Encyclopedia entry is especially useful here because it resists a strongly dualist reading: it says Julian has little of the body-soul dualism common among mystics, that God is in “sensuality” as well as “substance,” and that body and soul mutually help each other. That non-English reception aligns closely with the primary text.

4. Substance and sensuality: Julian’s core anthropology

The most important conceptual pair for extracting a philosophy of mind from Julian is substance/sensuality.

“Substance” is not physical stuff. It is the soul’s deepest created being as held in God. It is stable, loved, whole, and safe in God. God judges us according to this substance.

“Sensuality” is not “sensuality” in the narrow modern sense of erotic sensation. It is the soul’s lived, embodied, temporal, vulnerable condition. It is where we feel, fail, suffer, remember, repent, and undergo change. Julian says that in substance we are full, while in sensuality we fail; yet the failure of sensuality is not its rejection but the site of mercy and grace.

A recent Yale discussion usefully glosses Julian’s “sensuality” as our temporal, historical, bodily, and psychological soul-existence, while “substance” remains always in God and untouched by sin. That modern gloss is philosophically illuminating, as long as we remember that Julian herself is operating in medieval theological categories rather than contemporary phenomenology.

The crucial point is that Julian does not solve the problem of sin, embodiment, and mental instability by abandoning the lower part. She says the goal is for sensuality to be brought up to substance through Christ’s passion. The mind is healed not by escaping embodiment but by the redemption and integration of embodied consciousness.

This gives us a second major thesis: Julian’s philosophy of mind is integrative rather than eliminative. The lower, embodied, affective, sensory, and historical life is not discarded. It is re-ordered.

5. The triadic structure of cognition: truth, wisdom, love

Julian maps the soul’s activity onto Trinitarian properties. She says truth sees God, wisdom beholds God, and from these comes a holy delight in God, which is love. The human soul, as a creature in God, has these same properties “made”: it sees God, beholds God, and loves God. It is made for love.

This is one of the densest pieces of her philosophy of mind. It means cognition is not a neutral mirror that later adds affect. Seeing and beholding culminate in love, and love is not an optional emotional response to cognition but cognition’s proper fulfillment. Truth, wisdom, and love are not separate modules; they form a dynamic structure of mental life.

Julian therefore avoids both cold intellectualism and anti-intellectual affectivism. She does not say that love replaces understanding. She says that true understanding is fulfilled in love. This is why she can also say that reason is in God and is the highest gift, while also insisting that reason alone does not suffice without mind and love.

A concise philosophical formulation would be: for Julian, the mind is teleologically ordered cognition whose proper act is loving beholding.

6. Perception and revelation: bodily sight, inward word, ghostly sight

Julian’s own taxonomy of revelation is philosophically important. She distinguishes three modes: bodily sight, words formed in understanding, and spiritual sight. This gives us a layered theory of perception.

First, there is bodily vision, especially the sight of Christ’s Passion. This is not incidental. The mind receives truth through bodily and affective perception. Second, there is inward verbal cognition, where words are formed in the understanding without ordinary speech. Third, there is spiritual sight, which Julian says she cannot fully disclose as openly as she would wish.

A Spanish article by Victoria Cirlot adds an art-historical layer: Julian’s visionary images are connected to the iconographic and devotional practices of her period, especially Gothic Passion imagery designed to evoke compassion, while also producing novel and unusual images. This is important for philosophy of mind because it prevents two simplifications. Julian’s visions are not merely private mental images detached from culture, but neither are they merely passive reproductions of existing iconography. Her visionary cognition is culturally mediated, affectively charged, and theologically interpreted.

Julian’s epistemology is also disciplined by three supports: natural reason, the common teaching of Holy Church, and the inward gracious working of the Holy Spirit. She is not advocating unregulated private experience. Her mind-theory includes natural rationality, ecclesial authority, and inward illumination.

7. Self-knowledge: the soul cannot rest in itself

Julian’s doctrine of self-knowledge is subtle. She says the soul can never have rest in things beneath itself. Yet when it rises above all creatures and comes into itself, it still cannot rest merely in beholding itself; its beholding must be set in God, who dwells within it.

This is neither self-negation nor self-absorption. The self is real and noble; Julian even sees the soul as a vast, worshipful site, like an endless world or kingdom in which Christ sits. But the self is not self-explanatory. The soul knows itself by knowing the One in whom it is grounded.

In modern terms, Julian rejects both reductive externalism and isolated introspection. The self cannot be known merely from below, through bodily or worldly processes, nor merely from within, through self-inspection. It must be known through its ground. Her theory of self-knowledge is thus theocentric reflexivity: the self comes to itself by being turned toward God.

8. Desire, rest, and the intentional structure of mind

Julian’s famous hazelnut vision is a compact ontology of creation and desire. She sees a little thing like a hazelnut and understands that it is “all that is made”; it exists because God made it, loves it, and keeps it. She then says the soul cannot rest until it is “noughted” of all created things and rests in God.

This is not contempt for creation. The hazelnut is held in being by divine love. But created things cannot satisfy the soul as final ends. Julian’s philosophy of mind is therefore deeply teleological: the mind is structured by longing for its proper object. Desire is not an accidental mental state; it is the soul’s orientation toward rest.

Her account of prayer makes this even clearer. God tells Julian, in effect, that he is the ground of her beseeching: first God wills the gift, then makes the soul will it, then makes the soul ask for it. Julian concludes that beseeching is a new, gracious, lasting will of the soul, united and fastened to God’s will by the Holy Spirit.

Prayer, then, is not a mental act initiated by an isolated subject and sent outward to a distant object. It is a participatory act in which divine will grounds human will. Julian also says prayer “ones” the soul to God and is a witness that the soul wills as God wills.

Philosophically, this yields a strong thesis: the deepest intentional acts of the mind are co-constituted by God’s prior action. Human desire is not erased, but it is grounded in divine desire.

9. Sin as blindness, privation, and affective-cognitive disorder

Julian’s most difficult and philosophically important claims concern sin. In the “God in a point” revelation, she sees that God is in all things, does all things, and that nothing happens by chance from God’s perspective. She then says she saw no sin, and that sin is “no deed.”

This does not mean sin is unreal in lived experience. Julian repeatedly acknowledges sin, suffering, shame, contrition, and the Church’s teaching. Rather, she treats sin as privative and noetic: it is not a positive substance or divine act but a failure, blindness, and disorder in the creature’s condition. She says the cause of sin is blindness, because the person does not see God; if one saw God continually, one would not have sinful stirring.

This is a philosophy of mental pathology. Sin is not merely “bad behavior.” It is a distortion of perception, affect, and will. The soul falls into itself, loses sight and feeling of God, and experiences contrariety, tempest, sorrow, and fear.

Julian’s doctrine of wrath is equally radical. She says she saw no wrath in God, only in the human part. Wrath is a contrariety to peace and love, arising from failure of might, wisdom, or goodness—failures that cannot be in God. She later states even more strongly that if God could be wrathful even for a moment, we could have no life, place, or being.

The philosophical import is large: human affect can project onto God what is actually a disorder in the human soul. Wrath, dread, shame, and blame are not dismissed, but they are re-situated. They belong to the soul’s fallen sensuality and are healed by mercy and grace.

10. Divine judgment and human judgment

Julian distinguishes between God’s judgment and human judgment. God judges according to the soul’s “kind substance,” which is kept whole and safe in God; human beings judge according to changeable sensuality, which appears now one way, now another.

This distinction creates a profound philosophical tension. From the human standpoint, we are sinners and blameworthy. From the divine standpoint Julian is shown no blame. She explicitly struggles with this, saying her reason is greatly troubled because Church teaching and her own feeling tell her that sin involves blame, while in God she sees no blame.

Julian does not solve the tension by denying either side. Instead, she develops a two-perspective account of the person: the person as held in God’s eternal love and the person as historically changeable, fallen, and in need of mercy. Her philosophy of mind therefore includes a distinction between deep identity and phenomenal/moral condition. The deep identity is the soul’s God-kept substance; the condition is the soul’s changeable sensuality.

This is not a denial of moral seriousness. Sin remains terrible. But sin does not define the soul’s deepest ontological truth. That deepest truth is the “godly will” kept whole in Christ.

11. The “godly will”: moral identity beneath moral failure

One of Julian’s most distinctive psychological claims is that in every soul that shall be saved there is a “godly will” that never assents to sin and never shall. She also speaks of a lower “beastly will” that can will no good, contrasted with the higher will that always wills good.

This is not a simple split-personality theory. It is a metaphysical psychology of moral identity. The lower self is not unreal; it suffers, fails, sins, and requires mercy. But the higher will is the soul’s God-kept orientation to good. It remains intact not because the creature is morally impressive, but because God keeps the soul’s substance united to himself.

A modern reconstruction might call this a doctrine of deep volitional integrity under conditions of surface disorder. Julian thinks human beings can be morally and affectively disordered without their deepest God-given orientation being destroyed. That is why despair is a cognitive and spiritual error: it mistakes the fallen condition of sensuality for the final truth of the soul.

12. Mercy and grace as psychological healing

Mercy and grace are not external legal mechanisms in Julian. They are transformative operations within the soul’s life. Mercy keeps, suffers, quickens, and heals; grace raises, rewards, and turns shameful falling into worshipful restoration.

Mercy belongs especially to the maternal tenderness of divine love, while grace belongs to royal lordship, but both are one love. This is an important point: Julian’s “motherhood” and “lordship” imagery are not sentimental alternatives. They are complementary ways of describing divine action on and in the soul.

The result is that healing is not merely cognitive correction, not merely emotional consolation, and not merely juridical pardon. It is the restoration of sensuality to substance through divine love. The mind is healed when perception, will, affect, memory, and embodied life are reoriented toward God.

13. The mind of God: foreseeing wisdom, love, non-wrath

Julian’s account of the human mind depends on a robust account of divine mind, though “mind” must be used analogically here. God’s cognition is not discursive inference or deliberation under uncertainty. It is eternal foreseeing wisdom. Julian says that what appears to humans as chance or accident is due to human blindness and lack of foresight; to God, all things are ordered by foreseeing wisdom from without beginning.

God’s mind is also purposive and unchanging. Julian says God never changes his purpose, that nothing is unknown to him, and that everything is ordered before anything is made. This matters for human mentality because the soul’s deepest orientation is not toward an unknowable chaos but toward a providentially ordered reality.

Julian later summarizes divine properties as life, love, and light. Reason is in God; faith is a measured light in our night; at the end, the eye will be opened into full sight. She then identifies the light with charity and distinguishes charity unmade, charity made, and charity given: unmade charity is God, made charity is the soul in God, and given charity is virtue.

This yields a striking metaphysics of mind: created mind is made charity participating in unmade charity by given charity. Human cognition reaches fulfillment not by possessing neutral facts but by becoming luminous in love.

Philip Sheldrake’s recent article on Julian captures this well: true knowledge of God in Julian involves loving engagement rather than abstract intellectual inquiry, and Julian’s final theological understanding is that God’s meaning is love.

14. God as mother: divine mind as generative, wise, and nurturing

Julian’s maternal theology is central to her philosophy of mind because it defines the form of divine intentionality toward the soul. The Vatican’s French presentation of Julian quotes her teaching that Christ is our true Mother, that God is both Father and Mother, and that divine love surrounds us with protection and care. The Spanish and German Vatican audiences likewise emphasize that Julian compares divine love with maternal love, that God exceeds human love, and that her central message is that God is love.

Sheldrake’s analysis adds an important nuance: Julian does not merely say God is like a mother; she says God is our mother. Motherhood, in this reading, is not a human metaphor projected upward but a humanly visible expression of something first grounded in God.

For philosophy of mind, this means divine mind is not sheer surveillance, judgment, or command. It is generative wisdom, nourishing love, and patient formation. God’s knowledge of the soul is maternal in the sense that it knows the soul by creating, sustaining, feeding, correcting, and bringing it to maturity.

15. Christ’s soul and mind as the bridge between substance and sensuality

Julian’s Christology is indispensable for her philosophy of mind. She says Christ is the way who leads and presents us to the Father, and that the Second Person took our sensuality to deliver us. She also says that in Christ there are higher and lower parts, yet one soul: the higher part is in peace with God, while the lower part, sensuality, suffers for the salvation of humankind.

This is not an abstract doctrine of incarnation. It is the mechanism by which the human mind is healed. Because Christ takes human sensuality, sensuality can be brought up to substance. Because Christ’s soul is united with the divine, all saved souls are enclosed and restored in him. Julian says that where Christ’s blessed soul is, there is the substance of all souls that shall be saved.

Christ also sits in the soul. Julian sees the soul as an immense kingdom or city, with Christ seated in it in peace and rest; the place Christ takes in the soul he will never remove. Later she says God takes no resting place but in the human soul, which is his worshipful city.

So Julian’s philosophy of mind is not just theistic; it is Christologically incarnational. The mind’s integration is achieved through the incarnate Christ, whose own human soul unites the higher and lower, substance and sensuality, divine rest and human suffering.

16. Angels and nonhuman minds

Julian’s remarks on angels are sparse but important. She explicitly says she believes and understands the ministration of angels as “clerks” teach, but that it was not shown to her. She immediately adds that Christ himself is nearest, meekest, highest and lowest, and does all.

This should not be read as dismissal of angels. It is a careful epistemological ordering. Julian accepts angelic ministry within the wider Christian cosmos, but her revelation does not center on angelic mediation. Her anthropology is Christocentric: Christ, not the angels, is nearest to the soul.

Her earlier reference to the “blessed company of Heaven” also places saints and heavenly beings within the economy of help, while making clear that all such help is of God’s goodness. Angels also function as a standard of purity when Julian marvels that God shows no more blame to the soul than if it were as clean and holy as angels in heaven.

A respectful reconstruction would say this: Julian’s universe includes nonhuman spiritual minds whose ministry is real, but her own philosophical focus is not angelic psychology. Angels show that mentality is not exhausted by embodied human sensuality. Yet the human soul has a unique dignity because God takes his resting place there, and because Christ has taken human—not angelic—sensuality.

Any full Julian-inspired account of angelic mind would therefore need to combine Julian with broader medieval angelology. Julian alone gives us affirmation, hierarchy, and restraint, but not a detailed theory of angelic cognition.

17. Social and communal mind: “one soul” and the oneing of charity

Julian’s mind is not individualistic. Early in the text she says that if she looks at herself singularly she is nothing, but in general she is in the “onehood of charity” with all her fellow Christians; in that onehood stands the life of all humankind that shall be saved.

Later she says God loves all who shall be saved “as it were all one soul.” This does not erase individuality, but it means the deepest form of personal identity is communal charity. The soul is not fully understood as a solitary unit. It is understood in relation to Christ, to fellow Christians, to the Church, and to the whole saved community.

This is another point where Julian’s work can enrich philosophy of mind. She gives us neither modern individualism nor collectivist dissolution. Rather, she offers communion without erasure: the soul is personally loved, yet most truly itself in charity’s onehood.

18. What non-English reception usefully highlights

The non-English materials I found do not overturn the English-language scholarly picture, but they foreground emphases that are easy to underplay.

First, Spanish-language reception helpfully stresses Julian’s weak body-soul dualism. The Spanish Catholic Encyclopedia explicitly says God is in sensuality as well as substance and that body and soul mutually help one another. This is directly relevant for a philosophy of mind because it supports reading Julian as an embodied thinker rather than as a simple anti-body mystic.

Second, Victoria Cirlot’s Spanish article stresses the visual and affective culture of Julian’s revelations: the visions relate to Gothic Passion imagery and devotional practices designed to evoke compassion, while also producing new and unusual images. This helps frame Julian’s theory of cognition as image-mediated, affective, and historically embedded.

Third, French, Spanish, and German Vatican materials consistently foreground divine motherhood, providence, and the claim that God’s love is the key to Julian’s theology. For philosophy of mind, this means Julian’s “divine mind” should be read not as detached omniscience but as wise, maternal, providential love.

19. Comparison with modern philosophy of mind

Julian is not a Cartesian. The soul is not an isolated thinking substance sealed off from world, body, church, and God. It is embodied as sensuality, socially situated among fellow Christians, and metaphysically grounded in God.

She is not a materialist. The soul is made directly by God, not from created matter, and its deepest substance is in God.

She is not a simple substance dualist. Although she distinguishes body, soul, substance, and sensuality, her goal is not separation but oneing. The soul and body grow together; sensuality and substance are rightly called the soul because of their union in God.

She is not a mere introspectionist. The soul cannot rest in beholding itself. It must behold God dwelling in it.

She is not an anti-rational mystic. Reason is grounded in God and is one of the highest gifts, but it must be joined to mind and love.

The closest constructive label is: participatory embodied theistic anthropology. In more contemporary terms, Julian offers a theory in which consciousness is embodied, affectively structured, socially and ecclesially normed, teleologically directed, and metaphysically grounded in divine love.

20. Major tensions and open questions

There is a real pantheism worry because Julian sometimes speaks as though there is no difference between God and the soul’s substance. But she explicitly qualifies this: God is God, and the soul’s substance is a creature in God.

There is a real agency problem because Julian says sin is “no deed” and that God does all things. Yet she also teaches confession, contrition, penance, hatred of sin, and the need to discern good and evil by reason and grace. The best reading is privationist: sin is not a positive divine act but a disorder of creaturely seeing, willing, and feeling.

There is a real universalism question. Julian often restricts her statements to “those who shall be saved,” and says she was shown no other, while her “all shall be well” language presses toward a more expansive hope. Sheldrake notes that Julian struggles with how “all shall be well” relates to Church teaching about hell, though he also suggests the tension may be rhetorically managed.

There is a real angelological limit. Julian affirms angelic ministration but does not give a detailed account of angelic cognition. A comprehensive Julian-inspired philosophy of mind could include angels, but it would need to borrow from wider medieval sources.

There is also a risk of over-systematizing. Julian is not writing a scholastic treatise. Yet her long text contains enough conceptual structure to justify a philosophical extraction.

21. Concise thesis set: Julian’s philosophy of mind

A comprehensive reconstruction can be stated in these theses. 1. Created-mind thesis: The soul is created directly by God and not from pre-existing created matter. 2. Participation thesis: The soul’s substance is in God, but the soul is not identical with God; it is a creature in God. 3. Embodied-sensuality thesis: The soul includes sensuality, its embodied, affective, temporal, changeable life; God is present in sensuality as well as substance. 4. Integrative thesis: Salvation is not escape from sensuality but its restoration and elevation to substance through Christ. 5. Triadic cognition thesis: The soul’s proper acts are seeing, beholding, and loving; truth and wisdom culminate in love. 6. Affective-cognitive thesis: Feeling, longing, dread, hope, joy, and beholding are not merely subjective emotions but modes of spiritually meaningful cognition. 7. Reason-with-love thesis: Reason is grounded in God and is a high gift, but it cannot suffice without mind and love. 8. Self-knowledge thesis: The soul can know itself only by seeking itself in God, because God is nearer to the soul than the soul is to itself. 9. Prayer-intentionality thesis: Prayer is a God-grounded act of will in which God makes the soul will and ask what God wills to give. 10. Sin-as-blindness thesis: Sin is not a positive substance or divine deed but a failure of sight, a blindness in which the soul loses sight and feeling of God. 11. Non-wrath thesis: Wrath is not in God but in the human part; divine mind is unwavering love, peace, wisdom, and goodness. 12. Godly-will thesis: Beneath the lower disordered will is a godly will, kept whole in Christ, that never assents to sin. 13. Christological-integration thesis: Christ’s soul and Christ’s taking of human sensuality are the bridge by which human substance and sensuality are reunited. 14. Divine-mind thesis: God’s mind is eternal foreseeing wisdom, life, love, light, and charity; human reason and charity participate in this divine ground. 15. Angelic-ministry thesis: Angelic minds are real ministers in the Christian cosmos, but Julian’s revealed anthropology centers on Christ’s direct nearness to the human soul. 16. Communal-mind thesis: The soul is personally loved but fulfilled in the onehood of charity; God loves the saved as though they were one soul.

The resulting philosophy is neither a footnote to medieval mysticism nor a disguised modern theory. It is a coherent theological philosophy of mind in which the soul is created by love, known by love, wounded by blindness, healed by mercy, integrated by Christ, and fulfilled in charitable beholding.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
